rules: Immediately notify the user when help, authorization, account or service enablement, configuration, or deployment action is required.
Provide the blocked scope, reason, exact minimal steps, expected result, validation method, and resume point.
Do not attempt workaround paths, alternative services or accounts, unnecessary plugins, or repeated retries without explicit user approval.
Track every user-owned action in workflow/USER_ACTIONS.md and continue only unaffected in-scope work.
Before choosing an execution path, assess whether the agent can complete it reliably with current tools, permissions, and context.
If the agent cannot complete it reliably, or the user can complete it materially faster with a few direct steps, ask the user to act first.
Do not attempt a more indirect, complex, installation-heavy, service-dependent, or token-intensive path until the user reports that the direct action is also blocked.
After the user reports a blocker, choose the smallest viable alternative and explain any new permission, cost, security, or scope impact.
Do not use this protocol to offload normal local documentation, coding, testing, verification, or already-authorized reversible work to the user.

notes: Current workspace contained no files and was not a Git repository before workflow initialization.
Git author identity was configured and verified as QI QI <qiqi417813490@gmail.com> before the first commit.
CR-001 established a mandatory user-dependency escalation protocol for authorization, enablement, configuration, and deployment actions.
CR-002 established a user-first efficiency protocol when direct user action is materially simpler than an agent workaround.
CR-003 registered the user-provided GitHub repository as the formal origin for version management; the remote was empty when verified.
GATE-001 was explicitly approved by the user; Stage 0 v1.0 is the confirmed opportunity baseline.
OCR success criteria must be normalized in Stage 1 because 80% and 95% targets appeared in earlier discussion.
